beginner.variation: Now it has a spicy teriyaki glaze

wines.name: Chardonnay
wines.explore: The buttery oak of Burgundy Chardonnay mirrors the lemon butter sauce. The wine's body matches salmon's medium weight perfectly.
wines.whyBest: Chardonnay's body matches salmon's weight, and its buttery oak character echoes the lemon butter sauce.

wines.name: Cabernet Sauvignon
wines.explore: Cabernet's tannins chemically react with the oils in salmon to produce a bitter, metallic flavor. The wine's power buries the fish's delicacy.
wines.whyBest: Cabernet's tannins react with fish oils to produce a metallic flavor. A fundamental chemistry clash.

wines.name: Riesling
wines.explore: The bright acidity cuts through the butter cleanly, and the citrus notes pick up the lemon. A slightly lighter feel than Chardonnay.
wines.whyBest: Riesling is excellent, but Chardonnay edges it because the oaky, buttery character specifically mirrors the lemon butter.

varWines.name: Gewurztraminer
varWines.explore: The wine's lychee and ginger aromatics echo the teriyaki's ginger and sweetness. Its residual sugar balances the glaze.
varWines.whyBest: Gewurztraminer's lychee and ginger aromatics are a natural echo of teriyaki's flavor profile.

varWines.name: Chardonnay
varWines.explore: The oaky butter that was perfect with lemon butter now feels muddy against soy sauce. Vanilla from oak clashes with ginger.
varWines.whyBest: Oaked Chardonnay's butter and vanilla fight with soy and ginger. Too many competing rich flavors.

varWines.name: Riesling
varWines.explore: The off-dry sweetness tames the spicy heat nicely, and the light body doesn't compete. But you'd miss the aromatic echo Gewurztraminer creates.
varWines.whyBest: Riesling works well, but Gewurztraminer's exotic spice specifically mirrors teriyaki's ginger and star anise.

beginner.principle: Match weight first. Fish = medium-bodied wine. Then consider the sauce.
beginner.principleVar: When the sauce changes, the wine changes. Spicy-sweet glazes need aromatic whites with sweetness.

beginner.variation: Now topped with spicy soppressata and honey

wines.name: Sangiovese
wines.explore: The wine's tangy acidity locks into the tomato sauce. The earthy, cherry character complements mozzarella and basil.
wines.whyBest: Sangiovese and tomato share the same acidity profile. They evolved together on the Italian table.

wines.name: Sauvignon Blanc
wines.explore: The wine tastes thin and grassy against the warm cheese. The herbal notes feel out of place with tomato sauce.
wines.whyBest: Sauvignon Blanc's herbaceous character fights the mozzarella. Wrong region, wrong weight.

wines.name: Merlot
wines.explore: Perfectly pleasant. The soft fruit and medium body don't clash. But compared to Sangiovese, something is missing.
wines.whyBest: Merlot works but Sangiovese's acidity specifically mirrors tomato sauce. Merlot is pleasant but not magical.

varWines.name: Lambrusco
varWines.explore: The bubbles scrub the spicy fat from your palate, and the slight sweetness tempers the soppressata's heat while complementing the honey.
varWines.whyBest: Lambrusco's bubbles scrub the palate of spicy fat, and its sweetness tempers heat and complements the honey.

varWines.name: Sangiovese
varWines.explore: Still handles the tomato sauce. But without bubbles, the spicy fat lingers, and the honey makes the dry wine taste austere.
varWines.whyBest: Sangiovese still handles tomato, but Lambrusco is better because carbonation cuts through fat and sweetness matches honey.

varWines.name: Malbec
varWines.explore: The ripe plum fruit stacks with the honey to create a cloying wall of sweetness. By the third slice, your palate feels exhausted.
varWines.whyBest: Malbec's ripe plum stacks with honey creating cloying sweetness. You need freshness, not more richness.

beginner.principle: Pair the sauce, not the crust. Tomato = high-acid wine. Cheese = medium body.
beginner.principleVar: Spicy + sweet toppings need bubbles or off-dry wines to reset your palate.

beginner.variation: Now it's fried chicken with hot sauce

wines.name: Pinot Noir
wines.explore: The earthy, herbal character echoes the rosemary and thyme. Medium body doesn't overwhelm the meat.
wines.whyBest: Pinot Noir's earthy, herbal character echoes the herbs. Medium body matches chicken's weight. The textbook pairing.

wines.name: Chardonnay
wines.explore: The buttery richness complements golden roasted skin. Enjoyable, but the wine doesn't echo the herbs the way Pinot does.
wines.whyBest: Chardonnay is fine but Pinot's earthy, herbal notes mirror the herbs. A flavor echo white wine can't create.

wines.name: Shiraz
wines.explore: The wine steamrolls the chicken. At 15% ABV with dense, jammy fruit, all you'd taste is the Shiraz. The chicken disappears.
wines.whyBest: Barossa Shiraz at 15% is three weight classes too heavy. The chicken disappears behind the wine's power.

varWines.name: Champagne
varWines.explore: The carbonation scrubs fried oil from your palate. The acidity cuts through the hot sauce's heat. A modern classic.
varWines.whyBest: Champagne's carbonation scrubs fried oil from your palate, and high acidity tames hot sauce. A modern classic.

varWines.name: Riesling
varWines.explore: The off-dry sweetness tames the hot sauce. But without bubbles, the fried oil lingers more than with Champagne.
varWines.whyBest: Riesling's sweetness handles heat, but Champagne's bubbles do double duty: cutting through both oil AND capsaicin.

varWines.name: Pinot Noir
varWines.explore: The hot sauce burns right through Pinot's subtle cherry and earth. All you'd taste is heat and alcohol. A waste of Burgundy.
varWines.whyBest: Delicate Pinot Noir gets steamrolled. The hot sauce burns through its subtle fruit and leaves only alcohol.

beginner.principle: Roast chicken is the sommelier's best friend. Almost everything works. Match the herbs and cooking method.
beginner.principleVar: Fried food needs bubbles or high acid. Hot sauce needs sweetness or bubbles. Champagne + fried chicken is a modern classic.

beginner.variation: Now grilled chicken Caesar with anchovies

wines.name: Pinot Grigio
wines.explore: Light and crisp, it slips in behind the salad without competing. The neutral profile lets the Parmesan and lemon dressing do the talking.
wines.whyBest: Pinot Grigio's light body and neutral profile don't compete with the salad. It refreshes without imposing flavor.

wines.name: Viognier
wines.explore: Heavy and floral, it would feel like wearing a fur coat to the beach. The low acidity can't match the lemon dressing.
wines.whyBest: Viognier's heavy, floral, low-acid profile overwhelms the greens and clashes with the lemon dressing.

wines.name: Sauvignon Blanc
wines.explore: Herbaceous notes and bright acidity work nicely. But Marlborough's pungent intensity can compete with the salad rather than complement it.
wines.whyBest: Sauvignon Blanc works, but Pinot Grigio edges it because Marlborough's intensity competes rather than complements.

varWines.name: Vermentino
varWines.explore: The saline, sea-breeze character tastes like it grew up alongside anchovies. Because it did, on the same Mediterranean coast.
varWines.whyBest: Vermentino's saline, coastal character is a natural partner for anchovies. They grew up together on the same coast.

varWines.name: Sauvignon Blanc
varWines.explore: The flinty mineral character handles the anchovy's salt. But it lacks the saline, coastal echo that Vermentino creates.
varWines.whyBest: Sancerre works, but Vermentino is better because its Mediterranean salinity is a natural anchovy companion.

varWines.name: Chardonnay
varWines.explore: The oak and butter create a queasy combination with anchovy's intense brininess. Woody and fishy together.
varWines.whyBest: Napa Chardonnay's butter and oak clash with anchovy's brininess. An unpleasant combination.

beginner.principle: Salads need high-acid, light wines. The dressing matters more than the greens.
beginner.principleVar: Anchovies add umami and salt. Reach for coastal Mediterranean whites. They grew up together.

beginner.variation: Now a rich wild boar ragu with pappardelle

wines.name: Sangiovese
wines.explore: The wine's tangy acidity matches the tomato's tartness. The earthy, herbal character mirrors the slow-cooked meat. Italian grandmothers have served this for generations.
wines.whyBest: Sangiovese evolved alongside tomato-based pasta sauces in Tuscany. High acidity matches tomato, earthy character mirrors the meat.

wines.name: Pinot Noir
wines.explore: The heavy meat sauce buries Pinot's delicate cherry and earth completely. Like whispering in a loud room.
wines.whyBest: Pinot Noir is too light. The rich meat sauce overwhelms its delicate structure.

wines.name: Montepulciano
wines.explore: Rustic and full-bodied, it handles the sauce. But you'd miss the precise acidity match that Sangiovese creates.
wines.whyBest: Montepulciano works, but Sangiovese's acidity specifically mirrors tomato's tartaric acid profile.

varWines.name: Barolo
varWines.explore: The massive tannins bind to wild boar's proteins, softening the wine and tenderizing the perception of the meat. A legendary Piedmontese pairing.
varWines.whyBest: Barolo's massive tannins bind to the proteins in wild boar, softening both simultaneously. Gamey richness demands structural power.

varWines.name: Sangiovese
varWines.explore: Brunello has more power than Chianti and handles the gamey intensity better. But compared to Barolo, it doesn't grip the boar's richness as firmly.
varWines.whyBest: Brunello handles intensity better, but Barolo's tannin structure is specifically what wild boar needs.

varWines.name: Sauvignon Blanc
varWines.explore: The rich, gamey ragu would obliterate this wine entirely. Like bringing a bicycle to a truck race.
varWines.whyBest: White wine has no tannin. It gets completely overwhelmed by the ragu's richness and gamey intensity.

beginner.principle: Italian food + Italian wine. Tomato demands acidity. Meat demands body.
beginner.principleVar: Gamey, rich meats need powerful, tannic wines. The tannin-protein bond softens both the wine and the meat.

beginner.variation: Now peppercorn-crusted steak with blue cheese

wines.name: Cabernet Sauvignon
wines.explore: Tannins bind to the beef protein, making the wine smoother and the meat more tender. Fat carries Cabernet's cassis and cedar flavors deeper.
wines.whyBest: Cabernet's tannins bind to beef protein, making wine smoother and meat more tender. Fat carries the flavor deeper.

wines.name: Malbec
wines.explore: Plush, fruity, enough structure. Argentinians drink this with asado every day. But softer tannins create a less dramatic transformation.
wines.whyBest: Malbec is a great steak wine, but Cabernet's firmer tannin structure creates a stronger protein bond.

wines.name: Riesling
wines.explore: At 7% ABV with delicate sweetness, completely destroyed by the steak. Like pairing a whisper with a shout.
wines.whyBest: Mosel Riesling at 7% is featherweight. The steak obliterates it. Weight must match weight.

varWines.name: Syrah
varWines.explore: Black pepper in the wine meets black pepper on the steak. They contain the same compound (rotundone). The flavors literally speak the same chemical language.
varWines.whyBest: Syrah contains rotundone, the same compound as black pepper. Peppercorn crust + peppery wine = same chemical language.

varWines.name: Cabernet Sauvignon
varWines.explore: Still handles the steak well. But Bordeaux's restraint gets slightly overwhelmed by blue cheese, and you'd miss the peppery echo.
varWines.whyBest: Cabernet handles steak, but Syrah is better: pepper compound echoes the crust, and fuller body stands up to blue cheese.

varWines.name: Pinot Grigio
varWines.explore: Blue cheese alone would crush this wine before the steak arrives. Like drinking slightly acidic water next to very strong cheese.
varWines.whyBest: Pinot Grigio has neither the power for blue cheese nor the pepper for the crust.

beginner.principle: Red meat + tannic red wine. Protein softens tannin. Fat carries flavor. Wine pairing 101.
beginner.principleVar: Pepper demands peppery wine (Syrah). Blue cheese demands bold wine. Never pair delicate with powerful.

intermediate.dish: Pan-Seared Duck Breast with Cherry Reduction
intermediate.variation: Now it's duck confit with lentils

wines.name: Pinot Noir
wines.explore: The red cherry fruit mirrors the cherry reduction. Gevrey's earthy depth complements the gamey breast. Each bite makes the wine better.
wines.whyBest: Gevrey-Chambertin's cherry-fruited Pinot mirrors the reduction, while earthy depth complements the gamey breast.

wines.name: Grenache
wines.explore: Rich enough for the duck, soft tannins don't fight gamey meat. But you'd miss the cherry-on-cherry echo.
wines.whyBest: Grenache handles duck well, but Pinot's cherry fruit specifically mirrors the cherry reduction.

wines.name: Sauvignon Blanc
wines.explore: The duck's richness would flatten this wine instantly. You'd taste gamey meat with a thin, herbaceous afterthought.
wines.whyBest: Sauvignon Blanc is several weight classes too light. The gamey breast would flatten it.

varWines.name: Madiran
varWines.explore: Tannat is France's most tannic grape. Duck confit is France's fattiest dish. They evolved together in Southwest France. Tannin cuts through preserved duck fat like a knife.
varWines.whyBest: Tannat and duck confit evolved together in Southwest France. Tannin cuts through preserved duck fat like a knife through butter.

varWines.name: Cahors Malbec
varWines.explore: Darker and more tannic than Argentine Malbec. It handles the fat, but compared to Tannat, the tannin grip is slightly less firm.
varWines.whyBest: Cahors works, but Madiran's Tannat has even more tannin, which confit's extreme fat content demands.

varWines.name: Pinot Noir
varWines.explore: The preserved duck fat completely smothers Pinot's delicate fruit. Like trying to cut butter with a feather.
varWines.whyBest: Pinot Noir, perfect for seared breast, is too delicate for duck preserved in its own fat.

intermediate.principle: Duck is rich. You need either elegance that cuts through or structure that matches. The sauce tells you which.
intermediate.principleVar: Confit = preserved in fat. Fat demands tannin. Southwest French wines evolved alongside this dish.

intermediate.dish: Seared Scallops with Brown Butter and Capers
intermediate.variation: Now the scallops are in a Thai green curry

wines.name: Burgundy Blanc
wines.explore: Meursault's hazelnut and butter notes create a direct flavor echo with brown butter. The wine and the sauce speak the same language.
wines.whyBest: Meursault's hazelnut and butter notes from lees aging create a direct flavor echo with brown butter sauce.

wines.name: Muscadet
wines.explore: Salinity and mineral complement scallops and capers. But compared to Meursault, it feels lean against brown butter.
wines.whyBest: Muscadet works for shellfish, but Meursault's rich, nutty character mirrors brown butter. Muscadet is too lean for the sauce.

wines.name: Cabernet Sauvignon
wines.explore: You'd taste something metallic and fishy. Cabernet's tannins react with scallop proteins to produce a bitter, metallic off-flavor.
wines.whyBest: Cabernet's tannins react with shellfish proteins to produce metallic off-flavor. A fundamental chemistry clash.

varWines.name: Gruner Veltliner
varWines.explore: The white pepper spice echoes green curry's lemongrass and galangal. The herbal character and crisp acidity cut through coconut milk's richness.
varWines.whyBest: Gruner Veltliner's white pepper echoes green curry spices, and its crisp acidity cuts through coconut milk.

varWines.name: Riesling
varWines.explore: Off-dry sweetness handles the heat. But compared to Gruner, you miss the herbal, peppery echo with the curry spices.
varWines.whyBest: Riesling handles heat well, but Gruner's peppery, herbal character specifically mirrors green curry's aromatics.

varWines.name: Burgundy Blanc
varWines.explore: The buttery richness that was perfect with brown butter now fights coconut milk for the same flavor space. Two rich, creamy things competing.
varWines.whyBest: Meursault, perfect with brown butter, clashes with coconut milk. Two creamy flavors fighting for the same space.

intermediate.principle: Shellfish = no tannin, ever. Then match the sauce: brown butter calls for nutty richness.
intermediate.principleVar: Asian spice needs aromatic whites. Coconut milk needs crisp acidity. The dish tells you the style.

wines.name: Vermentino
wines.explore: Mediterranean fish + Mediterranean white. Vermentino's saline character echoes both the fennel's anise and the fish's brine.
wines.whyBest: Vermentino grows on the same coast where branzino swims. Its herbal, saline character mirrors fennel and sea-salt brine.

wines.name: Albarino
wines.explore: Stone fruit and salinity work. But Albarino is Atlantic, a different coastal profile. It works technically but lacks Mediterranean kinship.
wines.whyBest: Albarino's salinity works, but Vermentino's Mediterranean origin is a better geographic match.

wines.name: Syrah
wines.explore: Peppery tannins with delicate white fish create a metallic, unpleasant aftertaste. The wine's power buries the fish.
wines.whyBest: Syrah's tannins react with fish oils. One of wine pairing's fundamental chemistry clashes.

varWines.name: Bandol Rose
varWines.explore: Bouillabaisse was born near Marseille. Bandol rose is made 30 minutes away. The rose has structure of a light red, enough for the rich rouille and saffron broth.
varWines.whyBest: Bandol rose has structure for rouille and saffron broth, with freshness for fish stew. Made 30 minutes from where bouillabaisse was born.

varWines.name: Cassis Blanc
varWines.explore: The white wine of Cassis was literally designed by proximity for bouillabaisse. But compared to Bandol rose, slightly less body for the rouille.
varWines.whyBest: Cassis Blanc is the purist's choice. Bandol rose edges it because rouille's richness benefits from rose's extra body.

varWines.name: Vermentino
varWines.explore: Perfect with simple roasted branzino, but too lean for bouillabaisse's richness. The rouille and saffron broth need more weight.
varWines.whyBest: Vermentino, perfect with simple branzino, is too lean for rouille and saffron broth.

intermediate.principle: Simple fish = simple white. Pair the cooking method: raw = lightest, grilled = medium, roasted = fullest.
intermediate.principleVar: Bouillabaisse is a stew, not a fillet. Provencal rose bridges fish and sauce with the structure of a light red.

intermediate.variation: Now it's a lemon tart with meringue

wines.name: Banyuls
wines.explore: Fortified Grenache with dried cherry, cocoa, and coffee notes that share actual flavor compounds with dark chocolate.
wines.whyBest: Banyuls' cocoa and dried cherry notes mirror dark chocolate's flavor compounds. They share the same bittersweet character.

wines.name: Tawny Port
wines.explore: Twenty-year Tawny's caramel complements chocolate beautifully. But compared to Banyuls, the caramel direction is slightly different from chocolate's cocoa-cherry direction.
wines.whyBest: Tawny Port works beautifully, but Banyuls' cocoa notes overlap with dark chocolate more directly.

wines.name: Moscato d'Asti
wines.explore: At 5.5% with gentle sweetness, completely outgunned by dark chocolate's intensity. The chocolate makes the wine taste watery.
wines.whyBest: Moscato at 5.5% is outgunned by dark chocolate's bitterness. The wine tastes watery.

varWines.name: Sauternes
varWines.explore: Citrus peel notes echo the lemon curd, sweetness matches the meringue, and acidity keeps everything fresh. Triple harmony.
varWines.whyBest: Sauternes has citrus echoing lemon, sweetness matching meringue, and acidity keeping everything fresh. Triple harmony.

varWines.name: Moscato d'Asti
varWines.explore: Light, slightly sparkling, peachy-sweet. Charming but lacks the citrus-peel echo and botrytized texture that match the meringue.
varWines.whyBest: Moscato is charming, but Sauternes' citrus-peel character echoes the lemon curd and its richness matches meringue.

varWines.name: Banyuls
varWines.explore: Dark, intense, cocoa-inflected character feels completely wrong with bright lemon. Like pairing dark chocolate with lemon.
varWines.whyBest: Banyuls, perfect with chocolate, is too dark and heavy for a citrus dessert. Match the flavor family.

intermediate.principle: The wine must be sweeter than the dessert. If the dessert is sweeter, the wine tastes sour. The only absolute rule.
intermediate.principleVar: Match the dessert's flavor family: chocolate = dark fortified, citrus = lighter sweet wines.

intermediate.variation: Now baked Oysters Rockefeller with spinach and Pernod

wines.name: Muscadet
wines.explore: Lean, saline, mineral. Muscadet tastes like the ocean. Sur lie aging adds texture. No other wine disappears into the oyster's brine so seamlessly.
wines.whyBest: Muscadet's salinity, leanness, and mineral character mirror the ocean. No other wine disappears into the oyster's brine so seamlessly.

wines.name: Champagne
wines.explore: Wonderful with oysters. Bubbles and mineral character complement brininess. But Champagne adds a layer of toast that's one step removed from pure oceanic harmony.
wines.whyBest: Champagne is excellent, but Muscadet edges it because its saline character literally mimics the ocean.

wines.name: Chardonnay
wines.explore: Oak, butter, and tropical fruit actively clash with raw oysters. The wood tannins create an unpleasant woody note against the brine.
wines.whyBest: Napa Chardonnay's oak, butter, and tropical fruit actively clash with raw oysters.

varWines.name: Chablis
varWines.explore: Unoaked Chardonnay grown on Kimmeridgian limestone: literally ancient oyster shell fossils. More body than Muscadet for baked preparations.
varWines.whyBest: Chablis grows on ancient oyster shell fossils. More body than Muscadet for baked preparations. The terroir contains actual oysters.

varWines.name: Verdicchio
varWines.explore: Almond, mineral, saline. Verdicchio's weight handles the Pernod and spinach. But Chablis has that unique geological connection.
varWines.whyBest: Verdicchio works well, but Chablis' oyster-fossil limestone gives it unique mineral kinship.

varWines.name: Muscadet
varWines.explore: Perfect with raw oysters, now too lean for Rockefeller's richness. The butter, spinach, and Pernod need more weight.
varWines.whyBest: Muscadet, perfect raw, is too lean for butter, spinach, and Pernod in baked preparations.

intermediate.principle: Raw shellfish = the leanest, most mineral wine. No oak, no butter, no fruit. Just salt and sea.
intermediate.principleVar: Cooking shellfish adds weight. The preparation changes the pairing. Always.
